findings.title: Missing abstract
findings.explanation: The draft has no abstract. Every general-academic venue—journals, conferences, and preprint servers alike—requires an abstract to frame the problem, state the contribution, and summarize key results. Without one, the paper cannot be evaluated or indexed.
findings.fix: Add an abstract (150–250 words) that states the problem (convergence of iterative methods for linear systems), the specific contribution (a new or tighter spectral bound for the Jacobi method on SPD matrices), the main result, and its significance.

findings.title: No bibliography or references
findings.snippet:
Previous work have shown that such methods converges under certain conditions,
but our result are more tighter and applies to a broader class of problems.
findings.explanation: The paper cites no prior work at all. For any academic venue, claims like 'Previous work have shown…' and 'our result are more tighter' must reference specific publications so the reader can verify novelty and context. The absence of a bibliography makes the paper unpublishable.
findings.fix: Add a bibliography with references to foundational works (e.g., Varga's 'Matrix Iterative Analysis', Young's 'Iterative Solution of Large Linear Systems', Saad's 'Iterative Methods for Sparse Linear Systems') and cite them inline where prior results are invoked.

findings.title: Proof entirely omitted
findings.explanation: The paper's single theorem—its claimed main contribution—has no proof. Stating that details are omitted with no sketch, no reference to a supplementary appendix, and no citation to where the argument can be found is unacceptable at any academic venue. The reader has no way to verify correctness.
findings.fix: Provide a complete proof or at minimum a detailed proof sketch. If the bound is derived from standard spectral theory, make the key steps explicit: express B_J in terms of A, bound its eigenvalues using those of A, and derive the stated inequality.

findings.title: Claimed novelty is indistinguishable from known results
findings.snippet: The importent contribution of this work is that we provides a new bound on the convergence rate.
findings.explanation: The bound presented, ρ(B_J) ≤ 1 − 2λ₁/(λ₁+λ_n), closely resembles well-known spectral estimates for the Jacobi method on SPD matrices (related to the condition number κ = λ_n/λ₁). Without references, a comparison table, or explicit discussion of how this bound improves on existing ones, a general-academic reader cannot assess whether there is any actual novelty.
findings.fix: State the best previously known bound explicitly, cite its source, and show how the new bound is strictly tighter or applies under weaker hypotheses. If the result is not new, reframe the paper as expository.

findings.title: Unsupported sharpness claim
findings.snippet: The bound we derived are sharp and cannot be improved further without additional assumptions.
findings.explanation: Claiming a bound is 'sharp' is a precise mathematical assertion that requires proof or a matching lower-bound / extremal example. No such evidence is provided. At any academic venue, an unsubstantiated sharpness claim would be flagged by reviewers as overclaiming.
findings.fix: Either provide an example matrix (or family of matrices) for which the bound is attained, or weaken the claim to 'near-tight' with a quantified gap.

findings.title: Introduction lacks problem motivation, scope, and paper outline
findings.snippet:
This paper studys the convergance of iterative methods for solving linar systems.
The importent contribution of this work is that we provides a new bound on the convergence rate.
findings.explanation: The introduction is two sentences long with no motivation (why iterative methods matter), no description of the problem setting, no discussion of related work, and no outline of the paper's structure. For a general-academic audience, the introduction must establish context, clearly state the gap in existing knowledge, and preview the paper's approach.
findings.fix: Expand the introduction to ~1 page: (1) motivate iterative methods for large sparse systems, (2) survey existing convergence results with citations, (3) identify the specific gap or limitation, (4) state the contribution precisely, and (5) outline the remaining sections.

findings.title: Misspelled title undermines credibility
findings.explanation: While local typos are out of scope, two misspellings in the title ('Convergance', 'Iteratve') are a venue-level concern: the title is the first thing editors, reviewers, and readers see, and errors here signal a lack of preparation that would lead to desk rejection at most venues.
findings.fix: Correct to: 'On the Convergence of Iterative Methods'. Consider a more specific title that highlights the actual contribution, e.g., 'A Tight Spectral Bound for the Jacobi Method on Symmetric Positive Definite Systems'.
findings.rewrite: \title{A Tight Spectral Bound for the Jacobi Method on Symmetric Positive Definite Systems}

findings.title: Paper is far too short for a research contribution
findings.explanation: The entire document is roughly half a page of content. Even a short note or brief communication at a general-academic venue would typically be 4–6 pages minimum, with full proofs, examples, and numerical illustrations. The current length is closer to a homework solution than a research paper.
findings.fix: Expand the paper substantially: add a full proof, a worked numerical example, comparison with Gauss-Seidel or SOR, and discussion of practical implications.
